claim_text: Recently, Zhang et al. showed that the Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) metric, which uses the deep features of a pre-trained image-classification model to compute distances between images, correlated more strongly with human perception of image similarity than NRMSE, SSIM, or PSNR.

sub_claims.text: The Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) metric uses the deep features of a pre-trained image-classification model to compute distances between images.

evidence.section: Experiments.txt (Training on our data) / content.txt L41
evidence.snippet: For the lin configuration, we keep pre-trained network weights F fixed, and learn linear weights w on top. This constitutes a 'perceptual calibration' of a few parameters in an existing feature space. For example, for the VGG network, 1472 parameters are learned. For the tune configuration, we initialize from a pre-trained classification model, and allow all the weights for network F to be fine-tuned. Finally, for scratch, we initialize the network from random Gaussian weights and train it entirely on our judgments. Overall, we refer to these as variants of our proposed Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) metric.

We evaluate feature distances in different networks. For a given convolutional layer, we compute cosine distance (in the channel dimension) and average across spatial dimensions and layers of the network.

We evaluate the SqueezeNet [20], AlexNet [28], and VGG [52] architectures. We use 5 conv layers from the VGG network, which has become the de facto standard for image generation tasks ... We use the conv1-conv5 layers from [27].

evidence.section: Network architectures.txt (Computing distance from a network)
evidence.snippet: To compute a distance d0 between two patches, x, x0, given a network F, we first compute deep embeddings, normalize the activations in the channel dimension, scale each channel by vector w, and take the l2 distance. We then average across spatial dimension and across all layers.

deep networks, even across architectures (Squeezenet [20], AlexNet [27], VGG [52]) and supervision type (supervised [47], self-supervised [13, 40, 43, 64], and even unsupervised [26]), provide an emergent embedding which agrees surprisingly well with humans.

sub_claims.nuance: Zhang et al. explicitly name the metric 'Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS)' in the Experiments section, defined as deep embeddings (channel-normalized, channel-scaled, l2 distance, averaged across spatial dims and layers) over networks such as SqueezeNet/AlexNet/VGG that are pre-trained for ImageNet image classification. The lin and tune LPIPS configurations both initialize from a pre-trained classification model; only the 'scratch' variant does not. The manuscript's wording ('uses the deep features of a pre-trained image-classification model') matches the lin/tune configurations and the paper's headline framing exactly.

sub_claims.text: LPIPS / deep-feature perceptual distances correlate more strongly with human perception of image similarity than the traditional metrics NRMSE/MSE, SSIM, and PSNR.

evidence.snippet: the most widely used perceptual metrics today, such as PSNR and SSIM, are simple, shallow functions, and fail to account for many nuances of human perception... We find that deep features outperform all previous metrics by large margins on our dataset.

evidence.section: Evaluations.txt (How well do low-level metrics and classification networks perform?)
evidence.snippet: Averaged across all 6 test sets, humans are 73.9% consistent. Interestingly, the supervised networks perform at about the same level to each other, at 68.6%, 68.9%, and 67.0%, even across variation in model sizes - SqueezeNet (2.8 MB), AlexNet (9.1 MB), and VGG (58.9 MB) (only convolutional layers are counted). They all perform better than traditional metrics l2, SSIM, and FSIM at 63.2%, 63.1%, 63.8%, respectively.

We find that internal activations of networks trained for high-level classification tasks, even across network architectures [20, 28, 52] and no further calibration, do indeed correspond to human perceptual judgments. In fact, they correspond far better than the commonly used metrics like SSIM and FSIM [58, 62], which were not designed to handle situations where spatial ambiguities are a factor.

We show that deep features, trained on supervised, self-supervised, and unsupervised objectives alike, model low-level perceptual similarity surprisingly well, outperforming previous, widely-used metrics.

Classic per-pixel measures, such as l2 Euclidean distance, commonly used for regression problems, or the related Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), are insufficient for assessing structured outputs such as images, as they assume pixel-wise independence.

When validating on the traditional and CNN-based distortions (Figure 4(a)), we see improvements. Allowing the network to tune all the way through (brown) achieves higher performance than simply learning linear weights (purple) or training from scratch (pink). The higher capacity network VGG also performs better than the lower capacity SqueezeNet and AlexNet architectures... Across the 3 networks and 4 real-algorithm tasks, 11 of the 12 scores improved... However, these still outperform low-level metrics.

sub_claims.paper_value: Supervised deep nets ~67-69% 2AFC vs. l2/SSIM/FSIM 63.1-63.8% (humans 73.9%); LPIPS variants improve further over base deep nets and 'still outperform low-level metrics'.
sub_claims.claim_value: LPIPS correlates more strongly with human perception than NRMSE, SSIM, or PSNR.
sub_claims.nuance: Zhang et al. explicitly compare against l2 (closely related to MSE/NRMSE), PSNR (mentioned in Abstract/Motivation as a 'simple, shallow function' that 'fails to account for many nuances of human perception'), SSIM, and FSIM. The quantitative head-to-head in Evaluations is on l2/SSIM/FSIM (PSNR is monotonic in MSE/l2 so the ordering carries over). NRMSE is not named explicitly in the paper, but l2/MSE-family metrics are the head-to-head baselines that lose to deep features. The manuscript's wording matches Zhang et al.'s headline claim closely; one minor terminology gap is NRMSE-vs-l2/MSE.

phrasings_tried: feature distance / cosine distance / l2 distance over deep embeddings
human perception / human perceptual judgments / 2AFC agreement
PSNR / l2 Euclidean distance / per-pixel measures
outperform / correspond far better / better than traditional metrics

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Zhang et al. is the primary source: they introduce the LPIPS metric, the BAPPS perceptual judgment dataset, and the head-to-head against PSNR/SSIM/FSIM/l2. No indirect attribution issue.
attestation.out_of_context_check: Adamson 2025 cites Zhang et al. as the primary LPIPS reference and the empirical demonstration that LPIPS beats PSNR/SSIM/MSE-family metrics on human-perception correlation. This matches Zhang et al.'s actual scope (natural-image patches, 2AFC and JND tasks). Adamson 2025 applies the metric to medical/MRI images, which is outside Zhang et al.'s evaluation domain, but the manuscript sentence here is reporting Zhang et al.'s original finding (not claiming the result transfers to MRI), so this is in-context. The terminology gap of NRMSE vs. l2/MSE is minor and noted under sub-claim C028.b nuance.
